title: 3D Print Time Calculator by Layer Height 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: 3D Print Time Calculator by Layer Height
description: Estimate 3D print time using model height, layer height, print speed, and layer area. Get accurate hours for FDM printing. Free calculator.
intro: Before hitting print on a 3D model, you need to know: how long will this take? Can I run it overnight? This calculator estimates total print time by combining model height (mm), layer height (0.08–0.32 mm), print speed (mm/s), and average layer area (cm²). It's essential when balancing quality vs. speed: lower layer heights improve smooth curves but multiply print time; higher layers accelerate prototypes. Default values (0.2 mm, 60 mm/s) are the sweet spot for Ender 3, Prusa MK4, Bambu A1, and similar machines. For fast CoreXY printers (Bambu X1C, Voron), you can push 150–300 mm/s realistically.
keyTakeaway: A **100 mm tall model with 0.2 mm layer height and 60 mm/s** takes approximately **6–8 hours** depending on geometry. Doubling layer height cuts time by 40–50% but reduces surface quality.
useCases: You want to know if a print can finish overnight while you sleep.
You're comparing 0.2 mm vs 0.12 mm layer heights to choose quality vs. speed.
You're quoting jobs and need to estimate machine hours.
You're deciding if a fast CoreXY printer is worth the investment.
You're planning a batch of serial prints and need timing.

dataUpdate.notes: Industry-standard formulas for the proposed calculation.

example.title: 100 mm figure, 0.2 mm layer, 60 mm/s, 25 cm²/layer
example.result: With 25 cm² average area, the print takes ~27 hours. If you reduce to 10 cm² (slender figure): ~11 hours.

explanation:
## Formula

```
Layers = Height ÷ Layer_height
Extrusion_per_layer = Area_mm² ÷ (nozzle_width × layer)
Time_per_layer = (Extrusion_per_layer ÷ speed) × efficiency_factor
Total = Layers × Time_per_layer
```

Typical efficiency factor: 0.30–0.40. Printers accelerate/decelerate, retract, and travel between moves.

## Layer height: time vs. quality

| Layer height | Time multiplier | Quality | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.08 mm | 2.5× | Excellent | Miniatures |
| 0.12 mm | 1.7× | Very high | Detailed figures |
| 0.16 mm | 1.25× | High | Fine details |
| 0.20 mm | 1× ✅ | Good | General-purpose |
| 0.28 mm | 0.7× | Medium | Functional parts |
| 0.32 mm | 0.6× | Low | Fast prototypes |

## Real print speeds by printer

| Printer | Real speed |
|---|---|
| Ender 3 (stock) | 40–60 mm/s |
| Ender 3 + Klipper | 100–150 mm/s |
| Prusa MK4 | 200 mm/s |
| Bambu A1/P1S | 200–300 mm/s |
| Bambu X1C | 300–500 mm/s |
| Voron 2.4 | 250–400 mm/s |

## Variables not included

- **Cooling**: 100% fan speed may enforce a minimum layer time.
- **Supports**: Typically add 15–30% to total time.
- **Multi-color**: 60–180 seconds per filament change.
- **Warm-up**: 2–5 minutes for PLA, 10–15 minutes for ABS.

For total print cost, see [3D print cost calculator](/3d-print-cost-calculator) and [3D service hourly rate calculator](/3d-printing-service-hourly-rate).

faq.q: Why does my 3D printer take longer than this calculator estimates?
faq.a: Retractions, travels, direction changes, and minimum cooling add 20–50% to actual print time.

faq.q: What's the minimum layer height for good detail?
faq.a: 0.08–0.12 mm produces excellent detail. Most general-purpose prints use 0.16–0.20 mm.

faq.q: Does lower layer height always improve print quality?
faq.a: Yes for curves and smooth slopes (reduces visible 'stair-stepping'). Flat vertical surfaces show minimal difference.

faq.q: How does faster print speed affect quality?
faq.a: Speeds above 150 mm/s reduce time but require excellent tuning. Stock machines print best at 40–60 mm/s for consistent results.

faq.q: Can I use 0.4 mm layer height with a 0.4 mm nozzle?
faq.a: Yes, with larger nozzles (0.6–0.8 mm). Produces big functional parts 3–4× faster but with rougher finish.

faq.q: How much faster is Klipper firmware?
faq.a: Significantly: input shaping and pressure advance let an Ender 3 reach 150 mm/s without quality loss—3× faster than stock.

faq.q: How does resin printing (SLA/MSLA) compare in speed?
faq.a: Resin printing time is independent of part size: each layer cures in 2–4 seconds. A 100 mm part at 0.05 mm layers = 2,000 layers × 3 sec ≈ 100 minutes.

faq.q: Does bed warm-up time count toward total print time?
faq.a: Pre-heating (2–5 minutes for PLA, 10–15 for ABS) happens before printing starts and isn't included in layer calculations.

faq.q: What's the fastest realistic print speed for an Ender 3?
faq.a: Stock: 40–60 mm/s. With Klipper and tuning: 100–150 mm/s. Most users stay at 80–120 mm/s for reliability.
